# **Day 10 \u2014 Sorting Revision + Custom Comparators + Counting Sort / Radix Sort Intuition (README.rst)**  
*30 Days to DSA in C++ \u2014 Ultra\u2011Premium Edition*

---

## **1. Introduction**

Sorting is one of the most fundamental operations in computer science.  
It is the backbone of:

- Searching  
- Greedy algorithms  
- Binary search on answer  
- Hashing optimizations  
- Interval problems  
- Graph algorithms  
- Priority queues  
- Scheduling problems  

Today\u2019s goal is to master:

- Comparison\u2011based sorting  
- Custom comparators  
- Sorting complex structures  
- Stability in sorting  
- Counting sort intuition  
- Radix sort intuition  
- When to use which sorting technique  
- Interview\u2011level sorting problems  

This day strengthens your algorithmic intuition and prepares you for advanced topics.

---

# **2. Sorting Algorithms \u2014 Quick Revision**

### **2.1 Time Complexities**

| Algorithm | Best | Average | Worst | Stable | Notes |
|----------|------|---------|--------|--------|--------|
| Quick Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n\u00b2) | No | Fastest in practice |
| Merge Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | Yes | Used in stable sorts |
| Heap Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | No | Uses heap |
| Bubble Sort | O(n) | O(n\u00b2) | O(n\u00b2) | Yes | Educational only |
| Insertion Sort | O(n) | O(n\u00b2) | O(n\u00b2) | Yes | Good for small arrays |
| Counting Sort | O(n + k) | O(n + k) | O(n + k) | Yes | Non\u2011comparison |
| Radix Sort | O(d\u00b7(n + k)) | O(d\u00b7(n + k)) | O(d\u00b7(n + k)) | Yes | Works on digits |

---

# **3. C++ sort() \u2014 The Workhorse**

C++ uses **Introsort**:

- Quick Sort  
- Heap Sort  
- Merge Sort  

Time: **O(n log n)**  
Space: **O(log n)**  

---

## **3.1 Basic Usage**

```cpp
sort(arr.begin(), arr.end());
```

---

## **3.2 Sorting in Descending Order**

```cpp
sort(arr.begin(), arr.end(), greater<int>());
```

---

# **4. Custom Comparators \u2014 The Real Power**

Custom comparators allow you to sort complex structures.

---

## **4.1 Comparator for Sorting Pairs**

### **Sort by first ascending, then second descending**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](auto &a, auto &b){
    if (a.first == b.first)
        return a.second > b.second;
    return a.first < b.first;
});
```

---

## **4.2 Sorting Strings by Length**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](string &a, string &b){
    return a.size() < b.size();
});
```

---

## **4.3 Sorting Custom Structs**

```cpp
struct Student {
    string name;
    int marks;
};

sort(students.begin(), students.end(), [](auto &a, auto &b){
    return a.marks > b.marks;
});
```

---

# **5. Stability in Sorting**

A stable sort preserves the relative order of equal elements.

### **Stable Sort in C++**

```cpp
stable_sort(v.begin(), v.end());
```

Use when:

- Sorting by multiple keys  
- Sorting objects with equal primary keys  

---

# **6. Counting Sort \u2014 Intuition & Implementation**

Counting sort is a **non\u2011comparison** sorting algorithm.

### **When to use:**

- Range of numbers is small (0 to k)  
- k is not too large (k \u2264 10\u2076)  
- Input size is large  

### **6.1 Steps**

1. Count frequency  
2. Compute prefix sum  
3. Place elements in output array  

---

### **6.2 Implementation**

```cpp
vector<int> count(k+1, 0);

for (int x : arr)
    count[x]++;

int idx = 0;
for (int i = 0; i <= k; i++)
    while (count[i]--)
        arr[idx++] = i;
```

Time: **O(n + k)**  
Space: **O(k)**  

---

# **7. Radix Sort \u2014 Intuition**

Radix sort sorts numbers digit by digit.

### **When to use:**

- Numbers have fixed number of digits  
- Range is large  
- Counting sort is used as subroutine  

### **7.1 LSD Radix Sort (Least Significant Digit First)**

Sort by:

1. Units place  
2. Tens place  
3. Hundreds place  
4. \u2026  

Each step uses **stable counting sort**.

---

### **7.2 Implementation Sketch**

```cpp
int maxVal = *max_element(arr.begin(), arr.end());

for (int exp = 1; maxVal / exp > 0; exp *= 10)
    countingSortByDigit(arr, exp);
```

---

# **8. Sorting Complex Structures**

---

### **8.1 Sort Intervals by Start Time**

```cpp
sort(intervals.begin(), intervals.end(),
     [](auto &a, auto &b){ return a.start < b.start; });
```

---

### **8.2 Sort by Ratio (Greedy Problems)**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](auto &a, auto &b){
    return (double)a.first / a.second > (double)b.first / b.second;
});
```

---

### **8.3 Sort by Custom Priority**

Used in:

- Scheduling  
- Greedy  
- Activity selection  

---

# **9. Real\u2011World Examples**

---

### **Example 1 \u2014 Sort students by marks, then name**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](auto &a, auto &b){
    if (a.marks == b.marks)
        return a.name < b.name;
    return a.marks > b.marks;
});
```

---

### **Example 2 \u2014 Sort jobs by profit/weight ratio**

Used in fractional knapsack.

---

### **Example 3 \u2014 Sort points by polar angle**

Used in convex hull.

---

### **Example 4 \u2014 Sort intervals for merging**

```cpp
sort(intervals.begin(), intervals.end());
```

---

# **12. Solutions**

---

### **Solution 1 \u2014 Sort pairs**

Use comparator from section 4.1.

---

### **Solution 2 \u2014 Sort strings**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](string &a, string &b){
    if (a.size() == b.size())
        return a < b;
    return a.size() < b.size();
});
```

---

### **Solution 3 \u2014 Counting sort**

Use implementation from section 6.2.

---

### **Solution 4 \u2014 Merge intervals**

```cpp
sort(intervals.begin(), intervals.end());

vector<vector<int>> ans;
ans.push_back(intervals[0]);

for (auto &in : intervals) {
    if (in[0] <= ans.back()[1])
        ans.back()[1] = max(ans.back()[1], in[1]);
    else
        ans.push_back(in);
}
```

---

### **Solution 5 \u2014 Largest number**

```cpp
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](string &a, string &b){
    return a + b > b + a;
});
```

---

# **Day 10 \u2013 \u26a1 Sorting Revision + \ud83e\udded Custom Comparators + \ud83c\udfa8 Counting Sort + \ud83d\ude80 Radix Sort Intuition**  
`theory.rst`

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 1. Why Day 10 Is a Milestone**
Sorting is not just a topic \u2014 it\u2019s a **universal foundation** for:

- \ud83e\udde0 Greedy algorithms  
- \ud83d\udd0d Searching  
- \ud83e\udde9 Two pointers  
- \ud83c\udfaf Binary search on answer  
- \ud83e\uddee Hashing optimizations  
- \ud83c\udfc6 Competitive programming  
- \ud83e\uddf1 Data preprocessing  

Today\u2019s goal is to **master sorting beyond the basics** \u2014 to understand how to **control**, **customize**, and **optimize** sorting for real interview problems.

---

# **\ud83c\udf1f MEMORY ANCHOR: \u201cS\u2011C\u2011C\u2011R\u201d**
Today\u2019s four pillars:

- **S**orting Revision  
- **C**ustom Comparators  
- **C**ounting Sort  
- **R**adix Sort  

Remember: **S\u2011C\u2011C\u2011R** \u2192 \u201cSorting Creates Clean Results\u201d.

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 2. Sorting Revision (Deep, Visual, Memorable)**

---

## **\ud83d\udc99 2.1 Bubble Sort (Bubbles Float Up)**
- Adjacent swaps  
- Largest element \u201cbubbles\u201d to the end  

### **Time:** O(n\u00b2)  
### **Memory Anchor:**  
**Bubble = Swap neighbors until sorted**

---

## **\ud83d\udc9a 2.2 Selection Sort (Pick the Minimum)**
- Select minimum each time  
- Put it in correct position  

### **Time:** O(n\u00b2)  
### **Memory Anchor:**  
**Select \u2192 Swap**

---

## **\ud83d\udc9b 2.3 Insertion Sort (Sort Like Playing Cards)**
- Insert each element into sorted left side  

### **Time:** O(n\u00b2), but **O(n)** for nearly sorted arrays  
### **Memory Anchor:**  
**Insert where it belongs**

---

## **\u2764\ufe0f 2.4 Merge Sort (Divide \u2192 Conquer \u2192 Merge)**
- Recursively divide  
- Merge sorted halves  

### **Time:** O(n log n)  
### **Stable:** Yes  
### **Memory Anchor:**  
**M\u2011E\u2011R\u2011G\u2011E = Make Every Run Greatly Efficient**

---

## **\ud83d\udc9c 2.5 Quick Sort (Partition Around Pivot)**
- Choose pivot  
- Partition  
- Recurse  

### **Time:** Avg O(n log n), Worst O(n\u00b2)  
### **Memory Anchor:**  
**Quick but risky**

---

## **\ud83e\udde1 2.6 Heap Sort (Use Max Heap)**
- Build heap  
- Extract max repeatedly  

### **Time:** O(n log n)  
### **Memory Anchor:**  
**Heap \u2192 Highest first**

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 3. Custom Comparators (The Real Interview Weapon)**

Custom comparators allow you to **control sorting logic**.

---

## **\ud83d\udca5 3.1 Syntax (C++ Comparator)**

### **Function Comparator**
```
bool cmp(int a, int b) {
    return a > b;   // descending
}
```

### **Lambda Comparator**
```
sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int a, int b){
    return a > b;
});
```

---

# **\ud83c\udf1f MEMORY ANCHOR: \u201cR\u2011T\u2011S\u201d**
A comparator must define:

- **R**ule  
- **T**ransitivity  
- **S**table logic (not required but helpful)

If comparator violates transitivity \u2192 undefined behavior.

---

## **\ud83c\udfa8 3.2 Common Comparator Patterns**

---

### **\ud83d\udfe6 Sort by Value Descending**
```
return a > b;
```

### **\ud83d\udfe9 Sort by Absolute Value**
```
return abs(a) < abs(b);
```

### **\ud83d\udfe7 Sort by Pair (First asc, second desc)**
```
if (a.first == b.first)
    return a.second > b.second;
return a.first < b.first;
```

### **\ud83d\udfea Sort Strings by Length**
```
return a.size() < b.size();
```

### **\ud83d\udfeb Sort Strings Lexicographically**
```
return a < b;
```

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 4. Counting Sort (The Magical O(n + k) Sort)**

Counting sort is a **non\u2011comparison** sort.

---

## **\ud83d\udca1 When to Use**
- Range of numbers is small  
- Elements are integers  
- You need **stable**, **linear\u2011time** sorting  

---

## **\ud83c\udfaf Steps**
1. Count frequency  
2. Convert to prefix sum  
3. Place elements in output array (stable)  

---

## **\ud83e\udde0 MEMORY ANCHOR: \u201cC\u2011P\u2011P\u201d**
- **C**ount  
- **P**refix  
- **P**lace  

---

## **\ud83c\udfa8 Example**
Array: `[1, 4, 1, 2, 7, 5, 2]`

Count array (0\u20137):
```
0 2 2 0 1 1 0 1
```

Prefix sum:
```
0 2 4 4 5 6 6 7
```

Place elements \u2192 sorted.

---

## **\u26a1 Complexity**
- Time: O(n + k)  
- Space: O(k)  
- Stable: Yes  

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 5. Radix Sort (Sorting Digit by Digit)**

Radix sort uses **Counting Sort as a subroutine**.

---

## **\ud83d\udca1 When to Use**
- Numbers with fixed digit length  
- Strings of equal length  
- Large datasets where comparison sort is slow  

---

## **\ud83c\udfaf Steps**
1. Sort by least significant digit  
2. Move to next digit  
3. Continue until most significant digit  

---

## **\ud83e\udde0 MEMORY ANCHOR: \u201cL \u2192 M\u201d**
**Least to Most significant digit**

This ensures stability.

---

## **\ud83c\udfa8 Example**
Sort: `170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66`

### **Pass 1 (units digit)**  
Sort by last digit.

### **Pass 2 (tens digit)**  
Sort by tens.

### **Pass 3 (hundreds digit)**  
Sort by hundreds.

Final sorted array emerges.

---

## **\u26a1 Complexity**
- Time: O(d \u00d7 (n + k))  
- Space: O(n + k)  
- Stable: Yes  

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 6. Comparison Sort vs Non\u2011Comparison Sort**

| Type | Algorithms | Time | Stable | Notes |
|------|------------|--------|--------|--------|
| **Comparison** | Quick, Merge, Heap | O(n log n) | Merge is stable | General purpose |
| **Non\u2011Comparison** | Counting, Radix | O(n + k) | Yes | Only for integers / fixed length |

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 7. Sorting Pitfalls (Red Flags \ud83d\udea9)**

- \ud83d\udea9 Wrong comparator logic  
- \ud83d\udea9 Comparator not transitive  
- \ud83d\udea9 Using counting sort for large ranges  
- \ud83d\udea9 Forgetting stability in radix sort  
- \ud83d\udea9 Using quicksort for worst\u2011case inputs  
- \ud83d\udea9 Sorting strings without considering lexicographic rules  

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 8. Problems You Can Now Solve**
- Sort by custom rules  
- Sort pairs/structs  
- Sort strings by length/frequency  
- Counting sort problems  
- Radix sort problems  
- Bucket sort intuition  
- Top\u2011K frequent elements  
- Largest number formed from array  
- Sort colors (0,1,2)  
- Sort by parity  

---

# **\ud83c\udf08 9. What You Must Master Today**
- All comparison sorts  
- Custom comparator mastery  
- Counting sort implementation  
- Radix sort intuition  
- Stability vs instability  
- When to use which sorting algorithm  
- Sorting as preprocessing for bigger problems
